note: Each benchmark runs a REAL simulation and checks a measured number against a value physics/experiment already knows. If the lab is lying, these fail.

benchmarks.title: Water reaches its real density
benchmarks.why: SPC/E water at 300 K, 1 bar must settle near 1000 kg/m³ (experiment: 997). If the box, the ions or the barostat are wrong, this is the first thing that breaks.

benchmarks.title: Water's hydrogen-bond shell is in the right place
benchmarks.why: The first peak of the O–O radial distribution function is at 0.28 nm in real water (neutron scattering). This is the structure of liquid water itself.

benchmarks.title: The thermostat actually holds the temperature
benchmarks.why: Mean temperature must equal the setpoint. If it drifts, the integrator or coupling is broken and every result downstream is garbage.

benchmarks.title: Liquid argon is structured
benchmarks.why: A dense liquid has a strong first coordination shell: g(r) peaks well above 2. If this is flat, we are not simulating a liquid.

benchmarks.title: Argon gas is NOT structured
benchmarks.why: The same code at low density must give a nearly flat g(r). This is the control: it proves the previous test measured physics, not an artefact.

benchmarks.title: Quantum energy of water matches the textbook
benchmarks.why: Hartree–Fock/STO-3G on water is a canonical number: −74.96 Hartree. Any competent QM code reproduces it exactly.

benchmarks.title: The O–H bond comes out the right length
benchmarks.why: Scanning the bond must put the energy minimum at the real O–H distance (~0.96 Å). This validates the whole reaction-profile machinery.

benchmarks.title: A stable protein stays folded
benchmarks.why: Ubiquitin is a rigid, well-behaved protein. Backbone RMSD must plateau low (< 0.4 nm). A big number means the force field, ions or box are wrong — or it unfolded, which it should not.
